data.title: STOP Buying These 3 Dividend Stocks (And Buy These Instead)
data.description:
Co-written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor

Popularity does not equal quality.

There are plenty of popular dividend stocks that are not worth owning as long-term investments. Just as there are different reasons for buying a stock, there are many different reasons to avoid a stock as well.

Reasons to avoid a dividend stock include:

  • High payout ratios that may indicate unsustainable dividends (aka "yield traps")
  • A high debt load
  • A large wall of near-term debt maturities
  • A high cost of capital, including both debt (credit rating) and equity (stock valuation)
  • Highly cyclical revenues and cash flows
  • Lack of organic growth prospects
  • Lack of reasonably valued external/inorganic growth opportunities
  • Failure to rejuvenate growth even after multiple management changes
  • A major acquisition that increases debt or ventures into another business line outside the company's core competency
  • One's portfolio is already too heavily exposed to a certain sector or industry, such as energy, utilities, or real estate

A common mistake dividend investors make is owning a stock merely for the dividend.

"Who cares if there's high debt and no growth?" the investor thinks. "It pays a high dividend yield, and that's all I'm looking for."

The danger in this thinking is that high debt, lack of growth, and/or cyclicality can create a negative self-reinforcing loop in which lack of positive momentum aggravates the problems of debt, low growth, or cyclicality.

It keeps the valuation low, the cost of capital high, and reduces the company's ability to pull itself out of its self-reinforcing cycle of a weak balance sheet and poor growth prospects.

While there are some exceptional cases of companies that can remain fundamentally rangebound for a long period of time, it is generally the case that if a company isn't growing, it is dying.

It may be a rapid death or a slow death, but lack of growth tends to be a death knell all the same.

With that said, here are three dividend stocks we would avoid, followed by three others we would buy today.

3 Stocks To Avoid

1. Realty Income (O)

When it comes to popular dividend stocks, it doesn't get much more popular than O, the self-designated "Monthly Dividend Company."

On the surface, there's a lot to like about O, and there are good reasons why dividend investors have flocked to it over the last few decades.

It's a Dividend Aristocrat with a 30-year dividend growth streak. It enjoys an A- credit rating, which few other REITs can boast. And it has a proven track record of being a recession-resistant stock, given the nature of its long-term net leases that require tenants to pay for all or most property maintenance, insurance, and taxes.

Moreover, in the two decades leading up to 2022, O generated astoundingly strong total returns, beating both the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ):

What's not to like about this net lease stalwart?

For starters, O is sensitive to movements in interest rates and thrives most in a low interest rate environment. The REIT has a steady slate of debt maturities every year for the foreseeable future, and these loans will most likely be refinanced at higher interest rates, creating a refinancing headwind as far out as the eye can see.

Plus, higher interest rates and the resultingly lower equity valuation that comes with them together reduce the REIT's all-important investment spread between its cost of capital and its acquisition yields. This further reduces growth.

Notice that since 2022, O and the broader real estate sector have dramatically underperformed the S&P 500:

Rising interest rates were by far the biggest reason for this.

Another point to note about O is its sheer size. The REIT's portfolio is about $70 billion in total, making it bigger than the next 10 largest net lease REITs combined.

We think O has become a victim of its own success in multiple ways:

  1. Given the nature of its leases, O has minimal organic growth of no more than 1-1.5%. And the sheer magnitude of its portfolio makes it difficult to acquire enough real estate to generate external (acquisitive) growth.
  2. Given the need to widen the acquisition dragnet, management likely need to lower the underwriting standards of acquisitions or venture outside their sphere of competence for investments. We think both are happening.
  3. Lack of portfolio pruning in the past (in order to focus on growth) has resulted in overexposure to several risky tenants in the pharmacy, movie theater, and dollar store spaces.

With these unavoidable headwinds in view, we think investors would do well to avoid "The Monthly Dividend Company." While the REIT surely has a fantastic historical track record, we think its future will look much different than its past.

2. AT&T (T)

Since offloading its ill-conceived investment in Warner Media a few years ago, this telecommunications giant has taken significant steps to streamline its operations, reduce debt, and refocus on its core competency.

We applaud the company for these smart moves, and so has the market, having doubled the company's valuation over the last few years:

But virtually the entirety of T's rally over the last few years can be attributed to multiple expansion.

We admittedly don't know what the fair valuation should be for T, but we think it is well below its current ~13x forward P/E ratio. Historically, the company has traded at an 8-10x multiple. Even if you assume that the company is more valuable now that it has spun off Warner Media and reduced debt and that it deserves a 12x multiple, it would still be overvalued today.

Keep in mind also that T has not increased its dividend since the spinoff, and organic growth prospects are extremely limited going forward.

The US population is barely growing at all, and while T can push customers toward higher priced premium plans, it must also invest heavily in capex in order to maintain a competitive network. Even fiber, perhaps the fastest growth area for the company, is highly competitive, with many peers also investing heavily to capture market share.

While T's streamlined operations are an improvement from previous years, it remains a fundamentally low-growth company and should be considered as such.

3. Ford (F)

There has been lots of discussion lately about what extent this American automobile stalwart will benefit or be hurt by recent legislative and tariff policy changes.

But beyond the recent headlines, we simply think this stock is not a good long-term hold for dividend investors. The primary reason is because F is a highly cyclical stock.

When the economy is strong, jobs are plentiful, wages are growing, and asset values are rising, people buy cars. When the economy is weak, unemployment is high, wage growth is low, and asset values are in a downturn, people don't buy cars.

That may be a slight oversimplification, but only a slight one.

F has seen lots of ups and downs over the last two decades, but note that its stock price is at basically the same level as it was in 2005.

Moreover, the company's dividend has also been mostly flat over the last two decades:

Note that during recessions, the dividend is suspended entirely.

From 2007 through 2011, F paid no dividends. And from Q2 2020 through Q4 2021, F also paid no dividends.

Given this history, ask yourself what would cause the next 10-20 years to look any different.

Sure, F may slightly benefit from tariff protections and tax deductibility of auto loans, and removing the EV tax credit may also give a slight boost to its mostly ICE and hybrid vehicle offerings. But a recession anytime in the next several years could more than offset all of those benefits.

And, as we mentioned in the section about AT&T, the US population is barely growing, which removes a secular tailwind to car sales.

We think fans of Ford, especially dividend investors, should stick with the cars and not the stock.

3 Stocks To Buy Instead

1. NNN REIT (NNN)

NNN is another net lease REIT like O, but its portfolio is about 13% the size of O's. This gives NNN a significant amount of further room to grow its portfolio without running into the same size-related problems we discussed about O above.

Like O, NNN is a fellow Dividend Aristocrat with a 35-year dividend growth record.

And both REITs currently yield almost exactly the same 5.6%. But in several ways, NNN looks more attractive than O right now.

For one, NNN is valued at around 12.5x AFFO (adjusted funds from operations), whereas O is a turn higher at 13.5x AFFO.

At the same time, NNN's payout ratio is around 70%, whereas O's is a bit higher at 75%. That gives NNN a bit more retained cash to reinvest back into its business or deleveraging.

And while NNN and O have similar levels of leverage (mid-5x net debt-to-EBITDA ratios), NNN's balance sheet is more favorably structured. NNN boasts a weighted average debt maturity of 11.6 years, whereas O's weighted average debt maturity is 6.6 years.

This is a big reason why NNN has outperformed O over the last several years of rising or elevated interest rates.

In an environment where interest rates are no longer falling or ultra-low, NNN is the better investment.

That's why we prefer it to O.

2. Brookfield Infrastructure (BIP, BIPC)

BIP/C is an investment vehicle sponsored and partially owned by parent company Brookfield Asset Management (BAM). The company owns a diversified and global portfolio of infrastructure assets that provide stable, long-term, and inflation-linked cash flow streams.

BIP owns and operates ports, railroads, gas pipelines, toll roads, regulated utilities, intermodal containers, wireless towers, and data centers. These are economically essential assets, many of which benefit from global growth trends such as rising electricity demand, LNG exports, mobile data usage, and data center storage.

These assets typically feature long-term contracts with significant inflation protections. And the company's rapidly growing portfolio of data assets, especially data centers, is a critical piece of its future growth prospects. BIP is actively expanding its number and capacity of data centers to take advantage of the AI and cloud computing megatrend. The company plans to bring about 200 megawatts of data storage capacity online over the next few years.

BIP boasts a 16-year dividend growth record, and its 5.3%-yielding dividend looks poised to continue growing at a 5-7% annual pace going forward.

For dividend growth investors, this combination of a moderately high yield, mid-single-digit (or better) growth, and potential multiple expansion is the sweet spot for a great long-term investment.

3. Permian Resources (PR)

PR is a pure-play oil & gas producer operating in the Permian Basin, especially the Delaware Basin within the Permian. While smaller energy companies like PR do tend to be riskier than their supermajor peers, we think PR has several things going for it that mitigate risk.

Since the COVID-19 crash, PR's management team, especially the co-CEOs Will Hickey and James Walter who joined in 2022, have proven themselves to be extraordinary capital allocators.

They have reduced net debt to EBITDA down to about 1x, which fundamentally reduces risk for a company tied to the price of oil and gas. They have also opportunistically repurchased shares in the $10 range while remaining disciplined and careful with cash. On top of that, management have significantly hedged their balance sheet with oil hedging instruments that further reduces their cash flow volatility.

Aside from deleveraging and opportunistic buybacks, the new management team are steadily building a track record of strong acquisitions as well. For example, in 2023, PR acquired fellow Permian Basin E&P Earthstone in a stock-for-stock deal that turned PR into the second largest pure-play Permian producer. And more recently, PR used some of its accrued cash to acquire ~40,000 net acres of royalties from Occidental Petroleum (OXY).

With each acquisition like this, PR further increases its efficiency of operations and reduces its cost of production.

Also, the management team own over 6% of total shares, making them significantly aligned with shareholders.

The stock currently yields 4.4%, and management plan to allocate 50% of the company's substantial free cash flow to regular dividends going forward. The rest of FCF will go to opportunistic buybacks, acquisitions, deleveraging or special dividends.

We expect to see dividend growth begin over the next year or so.

Bottom Line

We love dividend investing. In fact, we make it our profession at High Yield Investor. But not all dividend stocks are equally attractive or advisable as long-term investments.

While there are some very compelling names out there, others are debatable, and others we wouldn't touch with a ten-foot pole.

Highly cyclical or heavily indebted companies, those with persistently low valuations (and high costs of capital), and/or those with a record of poor capital allocation strike us as being obvious stocks to avoid.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kaspi's Business Continues To Grow Despite Inflation Woes
data.description:
It was just in June that I last talked about Joint Stock Company Kaspi.kz (KSPI). I've made no secret I'm quite fond of the company, both because of their ability to generate income in Kazakhstan and because of their potential as they start to make moves toward Turkey.

The stock has been up somewhat since then, and now we have Q2 earnings released this week, which came in with pretty solid results once again. Between those new earnings, the reaffirmed outlook for the full year, and the details offered in the conference call, I thought it was worth taking another look at Kaspi.

Revenue Growth Continued In Q2

Despite the continued inflationary struggles in Kazakhstan post-COVID, and the high interest rates that go with it as the nation's central bank tries to get it under control, Kaspi's business within the country continued to fire on all cylinders, once again outpacing the previous year handily.

Q2 2024

Q2 2025

Revenue

$1.11 billion

$1.79 billion

Interest Expenses

($272 million)

($404 million)

Pre-Tax Net Margin

49.4%

33.3%

Net Income

$449 million

$479 million

(source: Ex. 99.1 from SEC release)

A lot of the growth is coming in lower margin segments, which is why the net margin is worsening. There are some bright spots to note though on the long-term front within Kazakhstan.

The high-interest rates mean that Kaspi is seeing deposits soar, up fully 207% year over year. While they can make money off depositors, the real hope is that when inflation normalizes and the interest rates come back down, those deposits will be moved into spending, and in Kazakhstan, spending generally means going through Kaspi one way or another, and at a higher margin.

E-commerce overall remained rather strong, but the largest growth was in the e-Grocery business. That's a relatively new business, and they're still scaling up to be available nationwide. But in just five major cities so far, Kaspi is reporting 57% growth year over year, and is now servicing some 1.1 million customers. That's good news, though e-Grocery is necessarily a lower-margin business. Kaspi says they plan to open two more warehouses for that business in the rest of this year.

Overall, Fintech business in Kazakhstan seems to be going pretty strong in spite of the inflation, and with the deposits, Kaspi has potential for some substantial growth when the problem subsides.

Expectation Is Continued Strong Results in Kazakhstan

The rest of 2025 for Kaspi is expected to be strong as well. Estimates are EPS of $3.07 and $3.56, respectively, for the last two quarters. That would give us a full year EPS of $12.49, and a forward P/E ratio of around 6.81. That's strong even for the financial sector, as the median is 10.52.

The forecast is for the Kazakh economy to accelerate through 2025, though inflation continues to remain high. The general strength in the economy should allow Kaspi to keep meeting its targets, and the company reaffirmed their outlook for the rest of the year. The expectation would be consolidated net income growth in the 15% range for the full year.

Kaspi is embedded in almost a ridiculous amount of overall commerce in Kazakhstan. There's really nothing like it, but the company has ambitions to grow that even more, with officers saying during the conference call that they believe Kazakhstan, on the whole, is "underpenetrated."

Turkey: The Risk But Also the Reward

Kaspi's business expansion into Turkey remains a work in progress. Hepsiburada (HEPS) came in with revenue growth, but continued losses, at least on a GAAP basis. It's no surprise that Kaspi's acquisition of the majority of Hepsiburada in January didn't immediately turn profitability around, but it remains a bit of a drag. That's not to say there's not growth potential here.

In the conference call, Kaspi said they're still working to get Hepsiburada's systems up to their standard. That doesn't happen in a few months. Hepsiburada is a big business in its own right, and Kaspi envisions expansion in Turkey going a lot further than just that.

Last time we talked about the plan to buy Rabobank's Turkish subsidiary. That subsidiary doesn't really do anything right now, but it comes with banking licensing rights in Turkey, which is worth quite a bit to Kaspi for their various fintech interests. Kaspi says they still expect that deal to close at some point in the second half of the year.

Kaspi officers are saying they believe Turkey is an "underappreciated opportunity." I'm perfectly willing to appreciate it, personally, but I think we also have to recognize that it comes with risks.

Though I think the Hepsiburada purchase made a lot of sense in the context of trying to get Kaspi into the rather large Turkish economy, the reality is that their situation is wildly different from Kazakhstan. It's a bigger economy, more people, and a heck of a lot more competition. Kaspi almost certainly will be able to use its experience to improve Hepsiburada's bottom line, and they will insinuate themselves into Turkey's economy with the Rabobank deal, but it remains to be seen just how successful they will be in doing that, or how much they might spend chasing after dreams of major Turkish market share.

Kaspi is big, they have a lot of potential, but Turkey is a tough nut to crack. I'm not faulting them for trying, but it's important to continue watching how their Turkish effort unfolds, and how much it ultimately costs, because whether or not we get a proper return from that investment is very much an open question, and likely will be for a while.

Still Comparatively Cheap, And Still High Yielding

In the end, I'm going to reiterate my strong buy rating on Kaspi, because I like their ability to generate cash flow in Kazakhstan, and I believe that will only grow as time goes on. I may be a bit more circumspect about Turkey, because that looks to be a long-term proposition, but the Kazakhstan business should more than make up for it.

Investors should keep a close eye on inflation in Kazakhstan, because once that's under control I expect margins to start improving again, though again certain segments like e-Grocery are just naturally going to be lower margin businesses.

With regard to Turkey, I'd recommend patience, as it may well be years before we start to see meaningful returns. The potential is there, but it's nowhere near a sure thing, and if Kaspi spends too much money chasing after it, that may be a drag on the company at least in the near term.

data.title: Above 6% Yield From GGN And Its Financial Instruments
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield

With markets looking overvalued for common stocks, we are shifting our focus to quality, publicly traded preferred stocks. We are increasing our exposure to fixed income instruments because we believe that this is where the unrealized potential for returns lies. Looking for a good balance between risk and return, we turned to closed-end preferred stocks. They have a high credit rating and offer stable income. We are particularly interested in long-term instruments with high ratings and profit potential. Today, we are looking at GAMCO Global Gold, Natural Resources & Income Trust (NYSE:GGN) and its preferred stock (NYSE:GGN.PR.B). If you are familiar with our articles, you may have read our article from February, but if not, you can review it and follow how the fund is developing.

Fund Overview

Founded in 2005, GGN has $782 million of total net assets and 156 million common shares at the end of the second quarter of 2025. The number of holdings is 229. Its non-leverage expense ratio is 1.3%. In the picture below are shown details of the GGN's portfolio:

56% of the fund's portfolio is in the "Metals and Mining" sector, and 32% in the "Energy and Energy Services" sector. Exxon Mobil Corp, Northern Star Resources Ltd, and Newmont Corp. are among the top ten holdings of GGN.

This CEF's dividend yield is 7.95% and the annualized distribution rate on NAV is 7.84%. GGN is paying its shareholders a $0.36 yearly dividend in the form of $0.0300 monthly distributions.

At the current moment, the market price of GGN is $4.53, with a NAV of $4.59 and a negative one-year Z-score of -0.39. It's currently trading at a 1.31% discount. It has a low leverage of 10%, and the only source of it is the outstanding preferred shares.

Here is a 5-year total return comparison between GGN (the orange line) and its peers:

Preferred Stock

GGN has one preferred stock:

GGN.PR.B is with a current price of $20.65 and a current yield of 6.05%. Its current stripped yield is 6.10%. The call date is passed, so it is callable anytime. GGN issued 4 million shares of GGN.PR.B on 05/02/2013. The stock pays cumulative distributions of 5% per annum, or $1.25 per quarter, in the form of $0.3125 per quarter. GGN.PR.B has one of the safest credit ratings - Moody's gives it an "A2" credit rating.

Conclusion

GGN offers stable income, credit quality, and can be considered a safe investment in the long term. With a credit rating of A2 and a current yield of 6.05%, GGN.PR.B is a suitable instrument for investors seeking a balance between security and stable income.

data.title: Ace River Capital Q2 2025 Quarterly Letter
data.description:
Dear investors,

For the second quarter of 2025, Ace River Capital Partners, L.P. (the "Fund") returned -5.33%. Over the same period, the S&P 500 (SPX) and Russel 2000 (RTY) Indexes returned +4.1% and +3.6%, respectively.

The fund underperformed this quarter, largely due to weakness across several core holdings despite no material changes to long-term business value. We remain focused on what matters: owning a handful of high-quality small-cap businesses with durable economics, competent management, and the ability to reinvest at attractive rates.

Strategy

My goal is to maintain a concentrated portfolio of small and micro-cap companies with unique advantages and growth potential within their respective industries. The aim is to hold them for the long term. I will be focused on companies that have tangible assets with a preference for unique real estate such as special purpose licensed locations (RICK), waterfront property and marinas (HZO), equity participation interest in mineral deposits and mining operations (VOXR). This strategy will usually avoid companies that are involved in software, finance or insurance. Finding such companies requires significant time and effort. Due to the scarcity of suitable opportunities, constraints on time and resources, and opportunity costs, I will take substantial positions in the companies that meet my criteria and pass my risk/reward assessment. I do not subscribe to wide diversification and only research and follow a limited number of companies deeply. I will not spend much time on a company if I am unwilling to take a substantial position and have the conviction to add to that position on weakness. This approach requires a lot of research and patience. There will be long periods of inactivity with few trades, and I will rarely hold more than five companies at a time. This strategy accepts short-term volatility in pursuit of higher, long-term, tax-efficient compounding returns.

Portfolio Updates

RCI Hospitality: RICK is the only publicly traded owner of adult nightclubs in the US. Currently they own 57 clubs across 13 states and an additional 12 sports-bar restaurants with the "Bombshells" concept. With few municipalities issuing new adult entertainment licenses these businesses function as local monopolies with excellent unit economics. These clubs have limited potential buyers with RICK establishing themselves as the buyer of choice for any club owners looking to sell. This provides a long runway for growth.

RICK continues to do what it does best-generate cash, reinvest it intelligently, and grow shareholder value. They remain the dominant consolidator in the gentlemen's club industry, a space with over 500 targets that meet their acquisition criteria and very little real competition. The most recent quarter showed steady operating results and strong free cash flow. The stock remains undervalued relative to normalized earnings power, especially given the capital allocation discipline this management team has shown.

Vox Royalty Corp: Vox continues to build value through disciplined royalty acquisitions and cash-flowing assets in Tier 1 jurisdictions. This quarter brought updates on multiple key projects advancing toward production. With more than 60 royalties, several near-term catalysts, and a capital-light model, Vox is well-positioned to grow free cash flow without the dilution or capex risk that plagues traditional miners. We continue to believe the market underappreciates the earnings power and embedded optionality in the portfolio.

MarineMax: HZO faced pressure this quarter as retail boat sales continue to normalize post-COVID, compounded by higher interest rates. That said, the company is quietly transforming its business model by increasing its mix of service, storage, and marina operations-higher-margin, recurring revenue streams that improve business quality and reduce cyclicality. The market seems focused on the next quarter. We're focused on the next few years. We added slightly to our position at what we believe are attractive long-term prices.

Looking Ahead

The market continues to reward a narrow group of large-cap tech stocks, while small-caps remain broadly ignored. That's fine with us. We think this disconnect is creating some of the best opportunities we've seen in years. Many of the names on our watchlist trade at deep discounts to intrinsic value. We're prepared to act when the setup is right.

We'll continue to stay patient, focused, and selective.

Fund Positions - In Order of Portfolio Weight

RICK, VOXR, HZO, DSHK

Thank you for reading. Please reach out for any reason.

Nicholas D'Agnillo

Nick@AceRiverCapital.com | 401-499-3935

data.title: Western Midstream Partners And Aris Water Solutions Deal Makes Strategic Sense
data.description:
August 7th was a very interesting day for shareholders of both Western Midstream Partners (NYSE:WES) and Aris Water Solutions (NYSE:ARIS). Shares of the former closed down 4.7%, while shares of the latter appreciated a whopping 19.3%. The reason for these movements stems from the fact that, after the market closed on August 6th, the management teams at both businesses announced that Western Midstream Partners will be acquiring Aris Water Solutions in a cash and stock deal valued at $1.5 billion. The total enterprise value of the transaction is about $2 billion.

Based on the data provided, I can understand why Western Midstream Partners is making this move. This will further increase the company's exposure to the produced water industry and paves the way for additional cash flows. At the same time, I also understand why the market punished shareholders of the business. In order for the deal to really be attractive, synergies that have been projected by management need to be realized. If they aren't, you could argue that, at least in one way, Western Midstream Partners is overpaying for Aris Water Solutions. Even so, I don't see this as a bad move. And based on how shares of both companies are priced right now, I do believe that both of them deserve a ‘buy’ rating.

Checking out Western Midstream Partners

Before we get into the terms of the transaction between the two businesses, I think it would be helpful to dig into the operations of each one. To start with, I am digging into Western Midstream Partners. According to management, the company, which is 44.7% owned by Occidental Petroleum (OXY), engages in the business of gathering, compressing, treating, processing, and transporting natural gas. It gathers, stabilizes, and transports condensate, NGLs, and crude oil. It also engages in the gathering and disposing of produced water. For those not familiar with the last of these, produced water is actually a byproduct associated with the production of crude oil and natural gas.

In the case of fracking, for instance, water is used in order to breakthrough shale for the purpose of extracting oil and gas. This water often carries harmful chemicals and materials, including the proppants That are comprised of sand, silicon, and/or ceramic. The water that comes out of this process is not safe for human consumption. So there's really only two ways to deal with it. The first is to take it and put it into salt water injection wells. And the second is to recycle it. This is particularly important when it comes to the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions, but we will get to that in a bit.

Over the years, Western Midstream Partners has done a fine job of growing operations. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Overall financial results have been a bit lumpy, particularly when it comes to net profits. But the general trend for cash flows has been toward additional growth year after year.

As time has gone on, management has made investments in growing the physical footprint of the business. This year, for instance, it expects to allocate between $625 million and $775 million toward capital expenditures, 66% of which will be dedicated to growth opportunities. Investments in growth in the past have allowed the company to increase its throughput. From 2022 through 2024, throughput for natural gas assets for the business expanded from 4,366 MMcf per day to 5,226 MMcf per day. And for the first half of this year, the 5,181 MMcf per day represents an increase over the 4,989 MMcf per day reported one year earlier. As the chart below illustrates, we have also seen growth in throughput for crude oil and NGL assets, as well as for produced water assets.

Although it has not historically been the lion’s share of the business, the produced water side of the business is important. From 2023 through 2024, adjusted gross margin for produced water assets grew from $286.1 million to $394.9 million. The figure in 2024 accounted for 11.7% of the total adjusted gross margin generated by the company. This represents an increase over the 9.8% that it was responsible for two years earlier. And for the first half of this year, the $202.7 million in adjusted gross margin generated by these operations translated to arise from the $193.1 million reported the previous year. That took its share from 11.5% of total adjusted gross margin to 11.7%. Clearly, management has deemed it important to continue growing this piece of the pie.

Checking out Aris Water Solutions

The management team at Aris Water Solutions describes the company as an environmental infrastructure and solutions business dedicated to helping customers reduce their water and carbon footprints. By focusing on the prolific Permian Basin, the company has grown into a rather sizable operation, with major firms like ConocoPhillips (COP) and Chevron (CVX) counted amongst its largest customers.

The business really focuses on a few different things in the water handling and recycling solutions industry. As the image above illustrates, it takes produced water from the more than 35 customers that it has, transports it through its pipelines, and either disposes of it by injecting it into salt water injection wells or recycling it and ultimately selling it back to some of these same customers so that they can reuse it for their operations.

Over the years, the business has seen some pretty strong growth. From 2021 through 2024, revenue grew from $321 million to $435.4 million. This came as total volumes dealt with by the company grew from 1,284 kbwpd to 1,548 kbwpd thanks to additional investments that the business has made into its network. And as revenue increased, profits and cash flows grew as well. Net income shot up from $1.7 million to $26.9 million. Operating cash flow rose from $70.2 million to $178.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $119.5 million to $172.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $149 million to $211.9 million.

Management is not going to be reporting financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year until August 11th. So right now, we only have results for the first quarter. During that time, revenue for the company was $120.5 million. That's up from the $103.4 million reported the previous year and can be attributed to a rise in volumes from 1,523 kbwpd to 1,750 kbwpd. With the exception of operating cash flow, which worsened year over year, all profitability metrics for the company that I looked at improved from one year to the next.

This deal makes sense… but it carries risks

Given the focus of Aris Water Solutions and the fact that Western Midstream Partners already has operations in this space, this transaction conceptually makes sense. But of course, details matter. According to the terms of the agreement, should the deal close, which is currently expected to be achieved by the end of this year, shareholders of Aris Water Solutions will receive 0.625 shares of Western Midstream Partners for each share of Aris Water Solutions that they own. Based on the closing price of Western Midstream Partners on August 6th, this works out to a price of $25.20 per share. But after shares of the company dropped 4.7% in response to the news, the effective price is now down to $24.03 per share. It is important to note that shareholders can choose to receive $25 per share in cash instead. But this is limited to $415 million in total payouts. Considering that the common equity of Aris Water Solutions is being valued at $813.1 million, this means that a lot of the deal will be in the form of stock.

There is a little bit of ambiguity here when it comes to the agreement. Based on my own estimates, the implied market capitalization for shareholders of Aris Water Solutions should be the $813.1 million that I just stated. But management has said that this transaction is worth $1.5 billion, or $2 billion on an enterprise value basis. We do know that the enterprise has $369.1 million in non-controlling interests. But that brings us up to a value of just $1.18 billion. In all likelihood, the difference between that and the $1.5 billion stated by management relates to a step-up in the value of the non-controlling interests. But management has not provided much in the way of detail here for us to dissect. For the purpose of this article, I will rely on their reported figures for the value of the transaction.

As you certainly know by now, this deal makes sense from a business model perspective. Both firms operate in this space, and it will pave the way for Western Midstream Partners to generate additional revenue and profits from the industry moving forward. It also makes sense from a geographical standpoint. In the image above, you can see the asset map for both enterprises put together. There is some overlap. In addition to this, the assets are well positioned to be able to generate synergies since the clustering of resources typically brings with it cost savings opportunities.

This transaction, which brings with it about 790 miles of pipeline, as well as produced water handling capacity of approximately 1,800 MBbls per day, 1,400 MMbbls per day of water recycling capacity, 16 MMBbls of recycled water storage capacity, and around 625,000 total dedicated acres, opens the door for Western Midstream Partners to capture $40 million in annualized synergies. Should these synergies be realized, the implied EV to EBITDA multiple for this transaction stands at 7.5.

Of course, synergies are never guaranteed to come to fruition. But they will be vital in determining whether this is just a mediocre transaction or a good one. In the chart below, you can see how shares of Western Midstream Partners are valued using historical results for 2024 and estimates for 2025. In that chart, you can also see how Aris Water Solutions is being valued.

There are some different scenarios here that I decided to look at. The first one when it comes to Aris Water Solutions involves synergies being realized for 2024, 2025, and 2026. What I am doing here is essentially adding on the implied value of synergies from this transaction as though it had been completed at the very start of 2024. The second way that I'm looking at this is under the assumption that synergies are not realized. For the purpose of my analysis, I assumed that the $1.09 billion that Western Midstream Partners is taking on in order to complete this transaction bears an annual interest rate of 6%. I am also using a 14.7% tax rate in the computation of adjusted operating cash flow. This is the same tax rate that Western Midstream Partners paid in 2024.

When compared to how Western Midstream Partners is valued, you can see that there are some instances, especially when using the EV to EBITDA approach, where Aris Water Solutions actually looks like a good deal for shareholders. But because of the interest expense that we are factoring in here, even the best scenario shows trading multiples that are higher than what Western Midstream Partners is trading for at the moment. And if we assume that synergies are not realized, then the purchase is certainly more expensive. It is likely the uncertainty regarding synergies here that has investors in Western Midstream Partners feeling a bit pessimistic right now.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I can understand why the market responded in the way that it did for both companies during trading on August 7th. This transaction certainly looks positive for shareholders of Aris Water Solutions. It has the potential to look positive for shareholders of Western Midstream Partners, but that hinges a lot on synergies that are ultimately realized. Because of how cheap both enterprises are, I actually view both of them in a favorable light. However, there is no denying that, if the deal does go through, buying into Aris Water Solutions is the better approach. The implied buyout price right now is $24.03 per share. That represents a premium of 1% over where the stock is currently trading. So in essence, buying it is like buying Western Midstream Partners, which I believe is a good opportunity, at a slight discount. Given these facts, I do believe that both companies deserve a ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: The Near-Perfect 7% Income Portfolio: My Blueprint For Financial Freedom
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this month, I read an interesting article titled "Here's a Better Plan For Retirement Than Social Security." As we have frequently discussed how Social Security in the future may not be enough to have a decent retirement, you can imagine how the title got me to quickly click on the article.

Nir Kaissair, founder of Unison Advisors and a well-known Bloomberg Opinion columnist, highlighted an important aspect of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act for those planning to retire in the future. He emphasized a statement from Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who posted on X (formerly known as Twitter) about the significance of new retirement accounts for kids in building wealth and owning a stake in America's future.

Even without being political, I think everyone who owns stocks in America will likely agree with that statement, as America's success in innovation, energy independence, consumer wealth, and other things has created tremendous wealth in various sectors.

I mean, look at its tech sector:

The idea is super simple. If there's one advantage the average kid in America has over almost everyone else, it's potential time in the market. Depending on what source one uses, I think it's fair to say the average male born in 2025 has a theoretical life expectancy of roughly 85 years, with a higher number for females.

As we all know what the stock market does over time, it's difficult to argue against an idea that could take some pressure off social programs that currently account for more than a third of federal spending:

Most people don’t want to touch Social Security, even though the system is obviously flawed. It’s not sustainable, for one. Social Security is the largest expenditure in the federal government’s deficit-plagued budget. When combined with Medicare, which provides health insurance for retirees, the two programs account for more than a third of federal spending. - Bloomberg

Nir Kaissar mentioned an idea he had two years ago, which is to give each newborn baby in the U.S. $10,000 for a tax-free investment account. If that capital grows by 7.5% per year (after inflation!), similar to the return of the S&P 500 over the past 100 years, it would turn into $1.8 million the moment the baby turns 70, more than any Social Security program can provide.

If we apply the 4% rule, which is based on the theory that retirees can sell some of their savings every year without running out of capital, the passive income would be huge, even if we ignore high-yielding investments:

The idea is that, as long as the growth on their savings exceeds 4%, which a competently invested portfolio should be able to achieve, they can sustain that level of spending indefinitely. By that math, a $1.8 million portfolio will generate $72,000 of income a year, about triple the amount the average retiree receives from Social Security. - Bloomberg

Moreover, for what it's worth, it's also cheaper, as spending $10,000 on 3.7 million babies every year would be a $37 billion bill, nothing compared to the $1.6 trillion the government will spend on Social Security this year.

So, needless to say, I think this is a fantastic idea that incorporates a few key things, including:

  • The government's increasingly dire financial situation.
  • America's terrific stock market history and the elevated odds that this may continue in the future.
  • Empowering financial independence for future generations.
  • Encouraging early financial literacy. While I doubt every baby will turn into the next Buffett, the odds that this will get more people into stocks are high.
  • It will align America's financial success with widespread prosperity. So far, it's fair to say most people without substantial financial assets missed out on the market's fantastic track record.
  • Once babies retire, there are multiple ways to generate cash, including selling some assets following the 4% rule.

Having said all of this, nobody reading this will benefit from this, not even if it's implemented. After all, we're (likely) all adults and largely responsible for our own financial well-being.

That's why I decided to use this opportunity to focus on the "end stage" of the investment process, which is creating a portfolio that generates cash.

Last month, I wrote an article on my slow transition, which includes fast-growing dividend stocks I may sell in the future, dividend stocks with decent yields and decent dividend growth that I may keep if they end up giving me a high yield on cost, and high-income stocks that I do not own but will buy in the future to generate the cash flow I need to retire.

In the second part of this article, I present some of my favorite stocks and ETFs that could create a fantastic portfolio generating at least 6-7% income, which should be enough for many to create substantial income on top of their Social Security cash flows without being forced to sell assets.

So, let's get to it!

A Thick Layer Of ETFs

Although it depends a bit on one's view on life, one can make the case that the older we get, the lower the risk of making mistakes in the market. After all, we have less time to erase our mistakes.

If an 18-year-old buys a stock that goes to zero, it's a waste of time and capital, but not a real problem, as that person is still a few years away from their first real job. Even at 30 or 40, we can make these kinds of mistakes.

However, once we're really dependent on our capital providing steady income, that's when it becomes super serious. Especially in my situation, I expect to get close to zero additional income from sources other than my own wealth.

I cannot afford to make mistakes.

In fact, because that is the case, I cannot even make big mistakes in the wealth generation process, but that's for a future article.

One easy way to limit our ability to make mistakes is by diversifying. The more stocks we own, the lower the standard deviation of our portfolio returns. As Meir Statman found in 1987, the biggest diversification benefits are visible when people own fewer than 30 unique stocks in a portfolio (see below).

That's why ETFs are so handy as they basically allow us to diversify without having to do any research into single stocks. According to Schwab's research, there are just two downsides to an all-ETF portfolio:

  • ETFs aren't actively managed (that could also be a pro).
  • No potential to beat the market. However, for income-focused investors, that's OK, as the goal isn't to build wealth but to benefit from past wealth creation.

Here are the pros:

  • Diversified very well among various securities
  • Generally low ongoing expenses
  • Niche options available if desired
  • Transparency on a daily basis into the makeup of the fund
  • Trading flexibility
  • Seeks to match index performance (minus fees and expenses)

When it comes to buying ETFs, I would keep it super simple, building a base of diversified dividend value, covered-call strategies, and preferred equity, which is a hybrid between equities and bonds, with a high correlation to government bonds but a different risk profile (as you can see below).

In this case, I like the VanEck Preferred Securities ex Financials ETF (PFXF), which has $1.8 billion in assets, a total expense ratio of 0.4%, and 107 holdings, with a focus on investment-grade companies and unrated firms with stellar track records and healthy balance sheets.

The current yield is roughly 8%.

Over the past 10 years, PFXF has returned 60%, massively outperforming longer-term government bonds. If we get a bottom in long-term bonds, I expect substantially more upside in PFXF and its peer ETFs.

While it always makes sense to own some bonds for safety and as a cash equivalent for retired investors, in this model portfolio, I'm using preferreds, as I simply dislike buying bonds and because I really like the risk/reward.

I would also buy income through covered call ETFs, which are actively managed ETFs, where managers sell covered calls on existing positions (that's the "covered" part).

By doing so, the ETFs receive a premium from option buyers. That premium is additional income on top of the dividends provided by the holding companies.

The higher the volatility of the underlying assets, the higher the premium on covered call sales can be.

Unfortunately, the risk is that if the underlying shares rise beyond the strike price, the ETF loses these shares. However, since they are covered, the downside is limited.

If the call option expires OTM and the trader maintains the position in the stock, they can potentially consider selling another call after the expiration. On the other hand, if the stock moves above the call's strike price, the call option is in the money - ITM - and will likely be assigned, requiring the covered call holder to deliver the shares of the underlying stock at the strike price. - Schwab

This also explains why investors who are not yet dependent on income should avoid covered call strategies in most scenarios. After all, their biggest advantage is long-term capital appreciation. Tools that could potentially cap these gains (even for income purposes) should be avoided.

For example, over the past 10 years, the S&P 500 has returned 251%. The Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD) has returned 80%. This number includes reinvestment of all proceeds. Without them, the return would have been -15%. For some, that's not worth the risk of buying a 14% yield.

One of the covered call ETFs I like is the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ). This ETF has a tech-focused portfolio, including some of the biggest stocks that currently dominate the tech-heavy QQQ ETF (QQQ) and even the S&P 500, as we can see below.

The ETF's main goal is to provide monthly income from selling options and investing in U.S. large-cap growth stocks. It wants to deliver long-term returns as close as possible to the Nasdaq 100 with a much more favorable volatility profile. It enjoys a gold-standard five-star ranking from Morningstar and has a fair total expense ratio of 0.35%.

While one can make the case that it's nuts to use tech stocks for covered calls, I would disagree for two reasons:

  • Tech stocks have higher volatility, which means the potential option-based income is higher.
  • I believe elevated valuations make it very likely that value stocks will outperform big tech. This could make it a great time to apply a covered call strategy. After all, if I'm right, the risks of shares getting called away are now lower.

Moreover, despite its covered call strategy, the ETF has returned 51% since inception, just 20 points less than the QQQ ETF. That's not bad, in my opinion.

Currently, JEPQ yields close to 12-13%, depending on option income.

I also like the Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO), which was launched in 2016 with the goal of owning high-quality stocks complemented by a covered call strategy. Although its total expense ratio is slightly higher at 0.53%, it also has a five-star rating from Morningstar, offers a monthly distribution, and includes some of my favorite stocks as top holdings.

It seeks to provide 2-3% annual income from dividend income and up to 4% from options income, meaning its total yield is close to 7%.

Because of its somewhat conservative options strategy, it has found a great balance between options risks and benefits, which has paid off handsomely. As we can see in the overview above, it has returned nearly 170% during the past 10 years, 30 points more than the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

With that said, I would also own the SCHD ETF.

The ETF, which has an expense ratio of just 0.06%, holds some of the market's most popular dividend stocks, including Chevron (CVX), PepsiCo (PEP), Altria (MO), Verizon (VZ), and The Home Depot (HD). In other words, many of the companies I and numerous others discuss on Seeking Alpha almost daily.

Currently yielding 3.9%, this ETF allows us to cover all of these stocks without having to pick winners ourselves. As long as we buy this ETF, we don't have to buy any of these picks as separate holdings unless there's a very good reason why we want to be overweight in a specific stock.

That's the power of a thick layer of ETFs. We buy safety and income and can use excess capital to add specific investments to individualize our portfolios.

Individual Stocks For Additional Benefits

This is the part where we can become even more creative.

After all, the market is home to tens of thousands of publicly listed companies. While I would argue that the overwhelming majority will never turn into serious wealth builders, there are many we can trust to get the job done.

Personally, in a retirement portfolio, I would add at least two BDCs. In this case, BDC stands for Business Development Company, which is a private lender that works with middle-market companies by providing capital and often resources to help the company grow.

While they are cyclical due to their dependence on mid-market credit strength, many offer elevated yields and are run by highly competent management teams. This includes Capital Southwest (CSWC), an internally-managed BDC with a yield of roughly 11.0% and a history of rapid growth in a rather small BDC niche that tends to be overlooked by its bigger peers.

These bigger peers include Blue Owl Capital Corp (OBDC), the BDC owned and managed by Blue Owl Capital (OWL), one of the fastest-growing private lenders in the United States. OBDC yields nearly 12.0%.

Then, I would own a ton of midstream companies. As almost every single one of my regular readers knows, I'm in love with this industry because of its importance in North America's energy industry, the fixed-fee structure that reduces commodity pricing risks, and the increasing focus on dividend growth.

In this area, I like countless companies, including C-Corps and MLPs that issue K-1 forms. Unfortunately, as a non-American investor, I cannot invest in MLPs.

Attractive MLPs are Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ET), and MPLX (MPLX), which I would all buy in a heartbeat if I could.

Some of my favorite C-Corps include Antero Midstream (AM), which I own, ONEOK (OKE), which I may buy in the not-too-distant future, and TC Energy (TRP), the Canadian natural gas midstream giant I wish I owned.

Compamy Structure Yield
Enterprise Products Partners MLP 7.1%
Energy Transfer MLP 7.4%
MPLX MLP 7.4%
Antero Midstream C-Corp 4.9%
ONEOK C-Corp 5.2%
TC Energy C-Corp 5.1%

I would also include some picks with lower yields, like Union Pacific (UNP), which yields 2.5%, with high-single-digit annual dividend growth and a path to double-digit dividend growth in the years ahead.

While UNP is a SCHD-like stock (if you know what I mean), I would still buy it, as it adds an additional layer of growth without sacrificing income and quality.

Investors can even own lower-yielding stocks like Visa (V), which yields just 0.7%, as long as they balance income and growth in a way that fits their personal goals.

After all, this is just a model portfolio.

Our options are endless. So are our personal differences. Some enjoy great health, others inherit millions, while others work paycheck to paycheck, struggling to invest every month.

For now, I think this portfolio is a fantastic base to build income:

Asset Type Yield Weighting
VanEck Preferred Securities ex Financials ETF (PFXF) ETF (Preferred Securities) 8.00% 20%
JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) ETF (Covered Call) 12.5% (avg of 12–13%) 10%
Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO) ETF (Covered Call) 7.00% 15%
Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) ETF (Dividend Equity) 3.90% 15%
Capital Southwest (CSWC) BDC (Stock) 11.00% 3%
Blue Owl Capital Corp (OBDC) BDC (Stock) 12.00% 3%
Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) MLP (Stock) 7.10% 5%
Energy Transfer (ET) MLP (Stock) 7.40% 5%
MPLX (MPLX) MLP (Stock) 7.40% 5%
Antero Midstream (AM) C-Corp (Stock) 4.90% 5%
ONEOK (OKE) C-Corp (Stock) 5.20% 5%
TC Energy (TRP) C-Corp (Stock) 5.10% 5%
Union Pacific (UNP) C-Corp (Stock) 2.50% 5%
  • Total weighting: 100%
  • Average gross yield: 7%

Needless to say, as I have mentioned before, this is purely experimental. While I have little doubt that this portfolio adds high-quality income, it owns a lot of midstream, BDCs, and even covered call exposure.

One could certainly make the case that I have lost my mind by adding so much midstream, but I think it makes for a great foundation. Given that it's super easy to bump this yield up to 8%, it's also easy to add growth-focused investments or diversify by adding other higher-yielding vehicles.

As I'm slowly shifting my portfolio from growth to income, I'm sure my future retirement portfolio will include a lot more companies with relatively low yields yet high yields on cost. This is a model portfolio for people who start from scratch.

I think it's near-perfect, but for many people, some tweaks may be necessary.

Takeaway

I have to say, the idea of giving every newborn a $10,000 investment account sounds insane. Yet it may be brilliant, as it leverages time in the market to solve America's retirement crisis.

However, so far, this is just a fictional idea.

Also, since most of us won't benefit from that, we need to build our own income-generating portfolios.

In this article, I covered my strategy and a great portfolio that can be used as a starting point.

A mix of high-yield ETFs (like PFXF, JEPQ, DIVO, and SCHD) for diversification and safety, plus individual stocks, like BDCs and midstream giants, for extra income.

The goal is to build a portfolio yielding roughly 7% and balancing growth and cash flow without relying solely on Social Security.

It's not one-size-fits-all, but I think it's fair to say it's a solid blueprint for financial independence. For what it's worth, if I had this portfolio, I could retire tomorrow, at least in Albania.

Needless to say, I don't plan on ever retiring, as I would still be doing what I do now if I had a bigger net worth.

So, all things considered, start early, diversify wisely, and let compounding do the heavy lifting.

And, don't forget, life's good.

Risks To Keep In Mind

As you can imagine, even a near-perfect portfolio comes with risks.

Covered call ETFs may underperform in strong bull markets as shares could get called away. That's why non-retired investors looking to grow income should avoid them (generally speaking).

BDCs and midstream stocks, while high-yielding, can be volatile and sensitive to economic slowdowns, inflation, or credit stress. However, for midstream companies, these risks tend to be subdued due to fee-based income.

Preferred equities often behave like bonds, meaning higher rates could pressure prices.

Meanwhile, taxes, especially for non-U.S. investors or those in higher brackets, can bite into real yields. Please always look into how investments can impact your taxable income. As an author, I cannot help you with that.

And while diversification helps, no portfolio is immune to drawdowns.

In general, that's why it's essential to understand your goals, risk tolerance, and tax situation before making any major moves.

I'm just here to provide food for thought. I cannot produce tailored portfolios for you, as much as I would like to.

data.title: U.S. Tariffs Roil Gold Futures, While Greenback Steadies
data.description:
Overview

Most of the dollar's late sell-off yesterday after the White House endorsed Stephen Miran to fill the remainder of Governor Kugler's term at the Federal Reserve has been recouped today. In mostly narrow ranges, the greenback is firmer against the G10 currencies but the Canadian dollar and sterling. The dollar is also trading with a stronger bias against emerging market currencies. China reports its CPI and PPI figures tomorrow, and next week's US July CPI is expected to have risen for the third consecutive month. In an otherwise quiet session, the gold futures market was roiled by news that US Customs will be imposing a tariff on one-kilogram and 100-ounce gold bars, which previously were exempt. There is still much confusion, and some continue to suspect a mistake was made. In the spot market, gold is firm and probing the $3400 area. A higher close today would be the sixth in the past seven sessions.

Outside of Japan and Taiwan, the large bourses in the Asia Pacific region fell today to pare this week's gains. Today's losses in India, facing a 50% tariff from the US, were sufficient to turn it lower on the week. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is holding on to a small gain near midday. If sustained, it would be the fourth gain this week, and it would recoup most of last week's 2.6% decline. US index futures enjoy a firmer bias. European benchmark 10-year yields are mostly 1-2 bp higher today. The 10-year Gilt yield is making new highs for the week, near 4.57%, a six-point increase on the week. The 10-year US Treasury yield is softer, slightly below 4.25%, up about five basis points this week. September WTI slumped to $63.20 to take out last month's low before recovering back above $64.00. Today is ostensibly the deadline that President Trump had given Russia to agree to a truce.

USD: The Dollar Index rallied from July 1 through August 1, and this week's pullback nearly met the 61.8% of the rally (~97.85). It is consolidating today and is holding below yesterday's high (~98.50). It recorded the North American session low yesterday after reports suggested Stephen Miran would be nominated to finish Governor Kugler's term at the Fed. Meanwhile, the event markets favor Waller to be Powell's successor. The US economic diary is light until next Tuesday's CPI. The headline and core rates are expected to have risen in July for the third consecutive month. At the same time, a consensus appears to be forming for a rate cut at the next FOMC meeting (September 17). The Fed funds futures are discounting about a 90% chance of a September cut compared with slightly less than 40% before the July employment report. The current effective Fed funds rate (weighted average) is 4.33%. The futures market puts it at 3.74% at the end of the year. It was near 4% before the jobs report, which was up almost 10 bp from before the FOMC meeting, which the market saw the Fed's hold at the end of July, despite two governor dissents, as hawkish.

Euro: The euro rose through the (61.8%) retracement of its losses since the July 1 high (~$1.1830) in early European activity yesterday and was turned back from almost $1.1700. It was sold back to nearly $1.1610 as European dealers were closing their books. It recovered to settle higher on the day after the Miran news. It is trading quietly today between $1.1635-$1.1680. Recall that the high set after last week's poor US jobs data was slightly below $1.16. Given that the euro rallied three cents off last Friday's low, some consolidation seems likely, especially ahead of the US CPI on Tuesday.

CNY: China's large external surplus (trade and current account) is understood to mean that the yuan is undervalued. Most calls to revalue it are missing two elements. The first is the broader context of the foreign exchange market. The IMF says it is around 15% undervalued, though some economists think it is closer to 20% below fair value. Consider that the OECD's model of purchasing power parity has the yen and euro almost 55% undervalued and the Canadian dollar 21% undervalued. In fact, the only G10 currency overvalued is the Swiss franc (~17%). The US has often sought to have other countries re-value rather than accept the stigma of a dollar devaluation. If the dollar were to return to fair value against the euro and yen, it would also likely be considerably less overvalued against the yuan. That is to suggest rather than a yuan problem per se, there is a dollar problem. The dollar's overvaluation explains more than the yuan's undervaluation. The second element missing from most discussions is the process by which the foreign exchange adjustment should be made. How are we getting from here to there? This goes largely unaddressed, whether one is talking about the euro, yen, or yuan. To re-value their respective currencies, do the advocates want central banks to sell Treasuries (or Agency bonds) and dollars? With deflation (and China's July CPI and PPI will be reported tomorrow, Saturday), are critics arguing that the PBOC should hike rates? And few of the calls for a revaluation of the yuan acknowledge that last month when the US dollar rallied, the yuan was the strongest currency in emerging markets and outperformed all the G10 currencies. More immediately, this week's range was set on Monday: ~CNH7.1765-CNH7.1960. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1382 (~CNY7.1345 yesterday, the lowest since last November). The absolute value of the change in the dollar's fix this week was almost 0.40%, the most since last November.

JPY: This week's range was set on Monday and Tuesday: ~ JPY146.60-JPY148.10. The dollar nearly covered the range yesterday (~JPY146.70-JPY147.70) and remains inside yesterday's range today. The five-day moving average crossed below the 20-day moving average yesterday for the first time in a month, which is a good proxy for the near-term trend. On the upside, the dollar must get above JPY148.25 to be notable from a technical perspective. Household spending disappointed, rising 1.3% year-over-year in June, about half of what economists projected in Bloomberg's survey and down from 4.7% in May. Yet the takeaway is really the improvement this year. Consider that outside an occasional statistical fluke, household spending, which is reported in real terms, has been falling from late 2022 through late last year. In December 2024-January 2025, the first back-to-back increase was posted in two years. With today's data point, the H1 average year-over-year increase was 1.4%. In H1 24, household spending fell by 1.8% on average, year-over-year. The current account surplus narrowed to JPY1.35 trillion from JPY3.4 trillion in May. Only a small part of this narrowing can be explained by trade. The trade surplus narrowed to JPY470 bln from JPY522 bln in May. The swaps market is pricing in 14 bp of tightening this year, down from 15 bp at the end of last week.

GBP: Sterling recorded the session high yesterday, almost $1.3450 after the Bank of England announced what appears to be a hawkish cut. It made a marginal new high today. The closeness of the vote (5-4) after an initial three-way split encouraged market participants to reduce the chances of another cut in Q4. That cut was fully discounted at the close of Wednesday, but it has been reduced by a little more than a quarter. In the statement, BOE officials seemed more attentive to the upside risks of inflation. The last push higher to session highs, was following the Miran news. Sterling settled above the 20-day moving average (~$1.3395) for the first time since July 23. The $1.3465 area marks the (50%) retracement of last month's pullback, which bottomed last Friday near $1.3140. The next retracement (61.8%) is around $1.3540.

CAD: The US dollar fray support around CAD1.3725. It houses the 20-day moving average and the (50%) retracement of the gains from the July 23 low near CAD1.3575. Nearby resistance is seen near this week's high (~CAD1.3810). The greenback is pinned near yesterday's lows. Canada's July employment data is the pre-weekend data highlight in North America. In H1 25, Canada has created almost 144k jobs (~183k in H1 24). Of these, about 56k were full-time positions (~66k in H1 24). The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for a 10k increase in overall jobs (83.1k in June). The unemployment rate may return to 7%, the post-pandemic peak reached in May. Ahead of the report, the swaps market is discounting around a 30% chance of a cut at the September 17 meeting. Barring a surprisingly poor report, expectations are unlikely to change much in response to today's jobs data.

AUD: The Australian dollar came within around 5/100 of a cent from the (61.8%) retracement of the losses from the July 24 high of the year (~$0.6625) to last Friday's low (~$0.6420). Yesterday's low was near $0.6490 was seen toward the end of the European session. It is trading in about a quarter-cent range so far today above $0.6510. The Australian dollar has held its own this week, gaining about 0.70%, the second strongest G10 performance this week behind sterling (~1.2%), even though a quarter-point rate cut is widely expected next week.

MXN: As widely expected, Banxico cut its overnight rate target 25 bp to 7.75%. The quarter-point reduction follows four half-point cuts in H1 25. The easing cycle began in March 2024, and in all, five quarter-point cuts were delivered last year. The swaps market sees the terminal rate around 7.25%. Before the central bank meeting outcome was announced, Mexico reported the sharp drop in headline CPI that was expected (3.51% vs. 4.32%). The core rate stands at 4.23% (4.24% in June). Banxico forecasts an average headline pace of 4.1% and 3.8% in the core in Q3. After reaching nearly MXN18.760 in late NY morning turnover, the dollar trended back toward the lows before and after the rate announcement. It settled near MXN18.6130. The greenback is a narrow range in this week's trough. Last week's low was almost MXN18.51. This week's low, set Wednesday, was about MXN18.5825. Meanwhile, the US dollar has been sold against the Brazilian real and approached the year's low set last month near BRL5.40. The low in H2 24 was closer to BRL5.37.
